surface.name: Modal Hello, world! through a first cloud function run
surface.selection_basis: The official Introduction directs new users to the Hello, world! example. That example labels itself a tutorial of Modal's core features and provides a 60-second setup route to a function executed on Modal infrastructure.

alternatives_considered.name: Introduction LLM inference example
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires a GPU image and Transformers, while the Introduction lists Hello, world! first among simple getting-started examples.

alternatives_considered.name: Modal code playground
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The Introduction presents it as an install-free interactive alternative, but the Hello, world! page provides the more explicit general first-run sequence.

entry_point.developer_intent: Create a Modal account and run the official Hello World example until its function executes in the cloud and returns streamed logs and outputs.
assumed_prior_state: No Modal account, workspace, API token, package, cloned examples repository, app, function run, or billing setup is assumed.

An existing GitHub or Google identity and a local environment with Python, pip, Git, and a shell are available; no signup, OAuth authorization, token creation, clone, or cloud execution was performed during research.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Not established end to end by public official documentation
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: No complete first-user route can be established because the required GitHub or Google identity-provider authorization and return-to-Modal transition are not enumerated by Modal's public documentation.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The public signup page exposes provider choices, but the account setup guide resumes after choosing a provider and does not specify the external consent, account-linking, or Modal workspace-completion states.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The required identity-provider and post-authorization account transition is not established by Modal's public documentation.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The Hello, world! tutorial later demonstrates a remote function executing on Modal and streaming logs and outputs, but a route beginning with no Modal account cannot be reconstructed without inventing hidden authentication transitions.

prerequisites.requirement: Use an existing GitHub account for the typical route; the signup page also exposes Google and SSO choices.
Create a Modal user account before running or deploying an application.
Proceeding with signup signifies agreement to Modal's terms of service.
Have Python, pip, Git, and a shell for the published package-install, repository-clone, and run commands.

primary_path.action: Open Modal's signup page from the account-setup guide.
primary_path.output: A Sign up for Modal page offers Continue with GitHub, Continue with Google, and Continue with SSO.
primary_path.success_signal: A supported identity-provider choice is visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The account guide identifies GitHub as the typical route and SSO as an enterprise feature that must be requested.

primary_path.action: Choose Continue with GitHub for the documented typical signup route.
details: Modal documents requested GitHub scopes as user:email and, for workspace invites only, read:org.
primary_path.output: The browser is sent to Modal's provider-authentication endpoint.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Modal's public docs do not enumerate the provider consent, account-linking, return, or workspace-completion screens required after this action.

candidate_path_gap.reason: After the undocumented account transition, the Hello, world! page says to install the Modal package with `pip install modal`, run `modal setup` to create an API token, clone `https://github.com/modal-labs/modal-examples`, enter the repository, and run `modal run 01_getting_started/hello_world.py`.
candidate_path_gap.impact: The documented post-account cloud execution cannot form an end-to-end first-user candidate while the mandatory account transition remains hidden.

branches.path: Select Continue with Google on the signup page.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the external identity provider but still crosses an undocumented authorization and return transition.

branches.path: Select Continue with SSO after arranging the enterprise feature with Modal.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an enterprise access dependency and is not the typical individual route.
Changes only the CLI invocation used to authenticate and create the API token.

friction_gates.description: A third-party identity provider is required, and the external consent plus return-to-Modal states are not documented publicly.
The public signup page states that proceeding signifies agreement to Modal's terms of service.
The typical GitHub route requests user:email and requests read:org when workspace invites require organization membership visibility.

time_to_first_success.value: The Hello, world! page says the example can be run on Modal in 60 seconds, but this claim is presented alongside an already completed free-account prerequisite.

sources.title: Modal user account setup | Modal Docs
sections_used: What GitHub permissions does signing up require?

sources.title: Hello, world! | Modal Docs
sections_used: Running our function locally, remotely, and in parallel

uncertainties.question: What exact provider consent, Modal account-linking, workspace creation, terms acceptance, and post-return states follow Continue with GitHub or Google?
uncertainties.impact: The mandatory account portion of the first mile cannot be reconstructed or counted.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Modal's official account guide names the provider route but does not enumerate the external and post-authorization transitions.

excluded_after_success: No complete first-success boundary is claimed while the account transition remains undocumented.

Changing the function, mapping over more data, model inference, fine-tuning, production deployment, secrets, volumes, web endpoints, and billing upgrades occur after the documented first remote function run.
